--- SOURCE 2: Interdisciplinary Stress Physiology - Universität Salzburg ---

FULL CONTENT:
![Universität Salzburg](https://www.plus.ac.at/wp-content/themes/typo-canvas-child-v2/assets/img/PLUS%20Loewe%20schwarz.svg "Universität Salzburg")

The University of Salzburg boasts six faculties with 34 departments and around 90 degree programmes in digital and analytical, natural and life sciences, social sciences and cultural studies, law and economics, and theology. Almost 18,000 students are enrolled in bachelor’s, master’s and doctoral programmes here.

Founded in 1622 by Prince Archbishop Paris Lodron and re-established in 1962, today the University of Salzburg is the largest educational institution in Salzburg.

In order to get the best out of your time in Salzburg, you are likely to consider other factors such as suitable accommodation, various food and sports options, interesting part-time jobs, and exciting events as well. We also support students with parenting responsibilities.

If you are one of our graduates but feel you don’t want to leave student life behind just yet, join our Alumni Club.

The University of Salzburg is a research institution well established in both local and international networks. It is a high-profile institution boasting a diverse subject offer. Around 2,000 academics are responsible for shaping this profile through their excellent research work at the University of Salzburg.

Research-based teaching is one of the trademarks of the University of Salzburg. It combines the versatile research services with an excellent teaching offer for all students.

From the wide range of degree programmes to student services, important semester dates to studying abroad: you can find all the important information about studying at the PLUS here.

The taster offers for prospective students, “Studieren Probieren” and “Studieren Recherchieren”, allow secondary school leavers to find out more about entry procedures and preparations for university life.

### Interdisciplinary Stress Physiology (DSP ISP)

![DSP sujet](/wp-content/uploads/2021/02/DSP-ISP_LOGO_13-04-2016_02.jpg "DSP-ISP_LOGO_13-04-2016_02.jpg")

![DSP sujet](/wp-content/uploads/2021/02/DSP-ISP_LOGO_13-04-2016_02.jpg "DSP-ISP_LOGO_13-04-2016_02.jpg")

## Abstract

**The DSP-ISP is focused on high quality research and education on stress responses from the cellular to the systemic level.**   
Stress, seen as adaptive responses to environmental challenges (stressors), was central to survival of each individual and, thus, relevant for selection processes during evolution. Therefore, all organisms – from the unicellular to the multicellular organisms, from algae to mammals – share fundamental response patterns to stressors.  Accordingly, the DSP-ISP will evaluate the common physiological mechanisms across species as well as species specific variations. The main perspectives are on energy-, oxygen, and metal-induced stress responses as well as on the impact of stress hormones on cognitive processing and emotional memory.

**The following topics will be addressed:**

--- SOURCE 3: Decoding phytohormone signaling in plant stress physiology ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164

SUMMARY:
The main phytohormones include abscisic acid (ABA), which regulates water conservation; jasmonic acid (JA) and salicylic acid (SA), which are crucial for defense against pathogens; and ethylene (ET), cytokinins (CKs), gibberellins (GAs), and brassinosteroids (BRs), which are involved in various growth and stress responses. These discoveries, including insights into hormonal crosstalk, have not only highlighted the complexity of signaling but also provided mechanistic understanding of how plants transduce stress signals and modulate gene expression to orchestrate adaptive responses (Jones, 2014, Singhal et al., 2021). For instants, ABA's regulation of stomatal closure, osmotic adjustment, gene expression, seed dormancy, root growth, and antioxidant defense contribute to plant resilience and survival under adverse environmental conditions, making it a key player in stress responses (Aslam et al., 2022; Ullah et al., 2024).

Review article

Decoding phytohormone signaling in plant stress physiology: Insights, challenges, and future directions
=======================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Naeem Khan

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envexpbot.2025.106099](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envexpbot.2025.106099 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S0098847225000164&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/)

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •The review identifies novel signaling pathways through which phytohormones mediate stress responses in plants. 
*   •It reveals extensive crosstalk between different phytohormone pathways involved in stress adaptation. 
*   •The review uncovers new potential targets for the development of stress-tolerant crop varieties. 
*   •The review highlights significant gaps in current knowledge and proposes future research directions to address these challenges. 

Abstract
--------

Phytohormones are pivotal regulators of plant stress physiology, orchestrating adaptive responses to environmental challenges and ensuring crop resilience. This comprehensive review explores the intricate signaling networks through which phytohormones operate, emphasizing their critical contributions to stress adaptation. Highlighting the complex crosstalk and feedback mechanisms among multiple hormones (viz. Abscisic acid (ABA), Jasmonic acid (JA), Ethylene, Cytokinin, Gibberellic acid (GA) and Brassinosteroids) and pathways, the review elucidates the role of phytohormones in shaping stress responses at various regulatory levels, from transcriptional to epigenetic regulation. Despite significant progress, deciphering phytohormone signaling networks presents formidable challenges, including inherent complexity and technical constraints. Addressing these hurdles requires interdisciplinary collaboration and innovative methodologies. The review outlines promising research directions aimed at unraveling additional layers of hormonal regulation and enhancing our predictive capabilities for engineering stress-tolerant plants. Overall, this review highlights the pivotal role of phytohormones in plant stress physiology and their implications for sustainable agriculture and food security.

------------------

![Image 10](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0098847225000164-ga1.jpg)

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S009884722500022X)
*   Next article in issue

Keywords
--------

Hormonal crosstalk

Transcriptional regulation

Signaling networks

Adaptive responses

Plant resilience

1. Introduction
---------------

Phytohormones, or plant hormones, are natural chemical compounds produced by plants to control several aspects of growth, development, and physiological responses. The main phytohormones include abscisic acid (ABA), which regulates water conservation; jasmonic acid (JA) and salicylic acid (SA), which are crucial for defense against pathogens; and ethylene (ET), cytokinins (CKs), gibberellins (GAs), and brassinosteroids (BRs), which are involved in various growth and stress responses. Serving as signaling molecules, they convey information about environmental cues and internal physiological status to coordinate adaptive responses, especially in the face of stressors. In stress responses, phytohormones play pivotal roles, regulating the expression of stress responsive genes and mediating crosstalk between different signaling pathways. For instance, abscisic acid (ABA) triggers stomatal closure to conserve water during drought ([Verma et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib136)), while jasmonic acid (JA) and salicylic acid (SA) activate defense mechanisms against pathogens and herbivores ([Khan et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib60), [Yang et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib146)). Phytohormones also modulate physiological processes like osmotic adjustment and antioxidant defense, aiding plants in tolerating stress conditions ([Matsui and Seki, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib84)). Moreover, they orchestrate intricate signaling networks, such as the interactions between ABA, JA, SA, ethylene (ET), cytokinins (CKs), gibberellins (GAs), and brassinosteroids (BRs), enabling plants to prioritize survival strategies amidst changing environments ([Clark and Donoghue, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib20)).

Understanding phytohormone signaling in stress physiology has yielded significant advancements and key findings. Recent advancements in phytohormone research, including breakthroughs in hormone profiling, signaling pathways, and the integration of omics technologies, have significantly deepened our understanding of stress physiology. These developments have illuminated previously unexplored aspects of hormonal regulation and interactions under stress conditions ([Iqbal et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib50), [Kaya, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib55)). Researchers have unraveled a web of interactions among hormones, revealing a sophisticated network that regulates stress responses with precision and flexibility ([Rensing, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib106)). These discoveries, including insights into hormonal crosstalk, have not only highlighted the complexity of signaling but also provided mechanistic understanding of how plants transduce stress signals and modulate gene expression to orchestrate adaptive responses ([Jones, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib51), [Singhal et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib120)). Despite these advances, several questions remain unresolved, particularly concerning the precise mechanisms of hormone crosstalk and the impact of emerging stress factors. This review aims to address these gaps by exploring current research trends, technological innovations, and the implications for future studies. Furthermore, advancements in hormone profiling, metabolic pathway analysis, and omics technologies have unveiled the dynamic nature of hormone regulation, shedding light on hormone biosynthesis, transport, and metabolism under stress conditions ([Matsui and Seki, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib84)). Live imaging techniques have allowed for the visualization of hormone dynamics, emphasizing the importance of localized signaling events and tissue-specific responses in stress acclimation ([Ruggiero and Fricke, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib107)). From an evolutionary standpoint, comparative genomics and phylogenetic analyses have provided insights into the adaptive significance of hormonal regulation across plant species ([Clark and Donoghue, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib20)). However, most studies have focused on isolated stress conditions, leaving significant gaps in understanding how plants integrate multiple stress signals simultaneously. Moreover, the predictive models of plant stress responses often fail to account for environmental variability and complex interactions at the molecular level.

Plant stress physiology, encompassing the study of stress perception, integration, and response, holds critical importance in both agricultural and ecological systems. In agriculture, understanding stress physiology is essential for developing plans to palliate the adverse effects of environmental stresses on crop productivity and quality ([Paes de Melo et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib93); [Todaka et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib128)). In natural ecosystems, it aids in predicting and managing the impacts of environmental disturbances on plant biodiversity and ecosystem functioning. By untying the mechanisms underlying plant stress responses, researchers can identify genetic traits and physiological processes that confer stress tolerance, paving the way for the development of stress-resistant crops and restoration strategies for degraded habitats ([Pandian et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib94), [Zhang et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib148)). Overall, plant stress physiology serves as a cornerstone for advancing agricultural sustainability, ecosystem resilience, and global food security in the face of increasingly unpredictable environmental conditions ([Ikram et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib49); [Lesk et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib73); [Tollefson, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib129)).

2. Role of phytohormones in stress signaling
--------------------------------------------

Plant stress responses involve the intricate interplay of several key phytohormones, each playing unique roles in coordinating adaptive mechanisms to cope with environmental challenges. Here is an overview of major plant hormones involved in stress responses ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#fig0005)).

![Image 11: Fig. 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0098847225000164-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (315KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0098847225000164-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (315KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S0098847225000164-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. **(A-C):** Visualization of phytohormone interactions, stress responses, and molecular signaling pathways. (A) Phytohormone Interaction Network – Network diagram showing interactions between seven key phytohormones (ABA, JA, SA, Ethylene, Cytokinin, GA, and BR). The figure quantifies the strength of interactions (from 0 to 1), with arrow color representing the type of interaction (e.g., blue for synergistic, red for antagonistic) and line thickness indicating the strength (0.5–0.9); (B) Stress Response Mechanisms – Bar plot quantifying the intensity of each hormone’s response to different stress factors: drought, pathogen defense, and growth regulation. The y-axis represents the intensity (ranging from 0 to 1) of the response, with colors indicating different stress categories; (C) Molecular Signaling Pathway – Schematic quantifying the signaling strength at various stages of the pathway (upstream, signaling, downstream), with point size reflecting the signaling intensity (ranging from 0 to 1) and color denoting the component of the signaling pathway. Data simulated based on insights from [Gupta et al. (2017)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib36), [Pierik et al. (2010)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib98), [Sewelam et al. (2016)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib115) and [Zheng et al. (2023)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib151).

### 2.1. Abscisic aid (ABA)

Abscisic acid (ABA) is extensively studied in plant hormones, particularly in stress responses like drought and salinity stress, where it plays a central role in orchestrating adaptive mechanisms. ABA stands out as a pivotal player in these stress responses due to its multifaceted roles. When plants face drought stress, ABA levels rise in leaves, triggering stomatal closure to reduce water loss through transpiration. This mechanism conserves water and maintains cellular hydration levels critical for drought survival ([Bano et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib8); [Tuteja, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib132)). ABA also regulates osmotic adjustment, enabling plants to maintain cellular turgor pressure and hydration status under water-deficit conditions. It promotes the synthesis of compatible solutes like sugars, proline, and amino acids, aiding osmotic adjustment and protection against dehydration ([Gupta et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib38)). Moreover, ABA modulates the expression of stress-responsive genes involved in physiological and biochemical adaptations to drought and salinity stress, enhancing stress tolerance. It regulates genes encoding proteins for antioxidant defense, ion transport, osmolyte synthesis, and stress signaling pathways ([Yoshida et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib147)). ABA also influences seed dormancy and germination, inhibiting germination under unfavorable conditions like drought or salinity stress to ensure germination occurs under optimal conditions ([Finkelstein, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib32)). Additionally, ABA influences root growth under stress, inhibiting primary root growth while promoting lateral root development and root hair formation ([Cheng et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib18)). Furthermore, ABA induces the synthesis of protective proteins and antioxidants to mitigate water deficit stress. It triggers the production of stress-responsive proteins like late embryogenesis abundant (LEA) proteins, dehydrins, and heat shock proteins (HSPs), stabilizing cellular structures ([Priya et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib101); [Shah et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib117)). ABA also regulates antioxidants like superoxide dismutase (SOD), catalase (CAT), and ascorbate peroxidase (APX), scavenging ROS and maintaining cellular homeostasis ([El-Yazied et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib28)). For instants, ABA's regulation of stomatal closure, osmotic adjustment, gene expression, seed dormancy, root growth, and antioxidant defense contribute to plant resilience and survival under adverse environmental conditions, making it a key player in stress responses ([Aslam et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib5); [Ullah et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib133)). Nonetheless, the long-term impact of sustained ABA signaling on plant recovery post-stress remains underexplored. While ABA-mediated stress responses are essential for short-term survival, they may hinder growth and reproduction over time, particularly in crop plants where yield is a critical parameter ([Ruggiero et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib108)). Future research should investigate how plants optimize ABA signaling to balance immediate stress resilience with recovery and productivity.

### 2.2. Jasmonic acid and salicylic acid

Jasmonic acid (JA) and salicylic acid (SA) are pivotal plant hormones involved in defense against biotic stresses, including herbivory and pathogen attacks. JA is primarily associated with defense against necrotrophic pathogens and herbivores. When plants face herbivore attacks or mechanical stress, they release volatile organic compounds (VOCs) and damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs), triggering JA biosynthesis. This hormone regulates the expression of defense-related genes, including those encoding proteins involved in synthesizing secondary metabolites such as terpenoids, phenolics, and glucosinolates, which deter herbivores and inhibit pathogen growth. Additionally, JA induces the production of proteinase inhibitors, interfering with herbivore digestive enzymes and reducing their feeding capability ([Howe et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib44)). Moreover, it promotes the synthesis of defensive proteins like thionins and lectins, which have antimicrobial properties and can inhibit pathogen growth ([Campos et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib14), [Li et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib74)). Conversely, SA primarily defends against biotrophic pathogens, which depend on living host tissue for their growth. When plants detect biotrophic pathogen infection, they produce SA in response to pathogen-derived molecules like microbial elicitors or effector proteins. SA regulates the expression of defense-related genes, particularly those associated with systemic acquired resistance (SAR), enhancing plant immunity against subsequent pathogen attacks. These genes encode pathogenesis-related (PR) proteins and enzymes involved in phytoalexin biosynthesis, inhibiting pathogen growth ([Fu and Dong, 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib33)). Additionally, SA can antagonize JA signaling, redirecting resources from defense against herbivores to defense against pathogens, a phenomenon known as the SA-JA antagonism ([Verbeek et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib135)). The crosstalk between JA and SA signaling pathways allows plants to prioritize defense responses based on the type of pathogen or herbivore encountered. When facing necrotrophic pathogens or herbivores, plants activate JA signaling, inducing defense responses tailored to combat these attackers. In contrast, during biotrophic pathogen infection, SA signaling is activated, triggering defense responses effective against these pathogens ([Liu et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib79)). This antagonistic crosstalk enables plants to allocate resources strategically, prioritizing defense responses against the predominant threat. However, the SA-JA antagonism poses challenges in agricultural applications, as enhancing one pathway can lead to vulnerabilities in another. For instance, while upregulating SA-mediated defenses improves resistance to biotrophic pathogens, it might render plants more susceptible to necrotrophic pathogens or herbivores. This trade-off underscores the need for integrative approaches to engineering stress resilience in crops, potentially leveraging hormone crosstalk modulators or dual-resistance traits ([Kazan and Lyons, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib56)). The interaction between JA and SA pathways can also involve synergistic interactions, leading to enhanced defense responses against a broad range of pathogens and herbivores under certain conditions ([Di et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib23)). Additionally, other hormone signaling pathways such as ethylene (ET) and abscisic acid (ABA) may intersect with JA and SA signaling, further modulating plant defense responses in a context-dependent manner ([Sabagh et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib109)). Furthermore, the dominance of JA or SA signaling is highly context-dependent and influenced by external factors like light intensity, temperature, and nutrient availability. Studies indicate that such environmental parameters can shift the balance of hormone crosstalk, leading to variability in defense outcomes across different species and growing conditions ([van Wees et al., 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib134)). Addressing these context-dependent factors is critical for translating findings from model plants to crops.

Understanding these hormonal activities provides valuable insights into crop enhancement. For instance, manipulating JA and SA signaling pathways through genetic engineering or application of synthetic analogs can enhance crop resistance to specific stresses. By boosting JA pathways, crops can be engineered to produce more effective secondary metabolites or proteinase inhibitors, improving their defense against pests and pathogens. Similarly, enhancing SA signaling can strengthen systemic acquired resistance, making crops more resilient to biotrophic pathogens. These approaches not only contribute to increased crop yield and quality but also reduce the need for chemical pesticides, promoting sustainable agricultural practices ([Mipeshwaree Devi et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib87), [Rai et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib104), [Zhang et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib149)).

### 2.3. Ethylene

Ethylene, a pivotal plant hormone, plays a crucial role in regulating various aspects of plant growth, development, and responses to both biotic and abiotic stresses. It serves as a multifaceted coordinator of physiological processes, governing phenomena ranging from seed germination and seedling growth to leaf and fruit senescence, as well as the abscission of leaves and flowers. Synthesized in response to a diverse array of environmental cues such as mechanical stress, flooding, and pathogen attack, ethylene operates as a signaling molecule, intricately modulating gene expression and cellular responses through complex signal transduction pathways ([Bakshi et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib7)). Among its well-known functions is the regulation of fruit ripening, where it facilitates the conversion of starches to sugars and influences the production of volatile compounds responsible for aroma and flavor ([Liu et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib79)). Additionally, ethylene mediates plant responses to various environmental stresses, such as drought, flooding, and salinity. During periods of water stress, ethylene acts to regulate stomatal closure and root growth, aiding in water conservation and the maintenance of hydration levels ([Dubois et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib26)). Conversely, in flooded conditions, ethylene accumulation stimulates the expression of genes involved in aerenchyma formation and adventitious root growth, thereby facilitating oxygen transport to submerged tissues ([Sasidharan et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0098847225000164#bib113)). Furthermore, ethylene plays a critical role in plant defense against pathogens and herbivores by inducing the production of defense-related compounds such as phytoalexins, pathogenesis-related proteins, and volatile organic compounds ([Broekaert et al., 2018](https://www.

--- SOURCE 4: Interdisciplinary Plant Sciences: Integrating Plant Science Research ... ---
URL: https://plantae.org/interdisciplinary-plant-sciences/

SUMMARY:
Interdisciplinary research confers a multitude of advantages, acting as a pivotal conduit for the generative recombination processes that propel scientific

FULL CONTENT:
![Plantae](https://plantae.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/10/ASPB-LOGO-Full-Name-RGB-1.png "ASPB-LOGO-Full-Name-RGB (1)")
![Plantae Logo](https://plantae.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/10/Plantae_Logo_Main-240x49-1.png)

# Interdisciplinary Plant Sciences: Integrating Plant Science Research in Other Scientific Fields

The concept of ‘interdisciplinarity’ has emerged as a salient term within the scientific discourse, signifying an escalated interest in both the promotion of interdisciplinary methodologies and the evaluation of their efficacy. The scientific community increasingly recognizes interdisciplinary collaboration as a vital enhancement to scientific inquiry and a judicious career maneuver, receiving substantial support from funding agencies and policymakers. This is exemplified by the dedicated issue on interdisciplinary research published by Nature in 2015 and the thematic emphasis at the 5th annual meeting of the Global Research Council. Nation-states have implemented specific policy measures to underwrite interdisciplinary research endeavors, exemplified by the establishment of programs such as the National Academies Keck Futures Initiative (NAKFI) and the Integrative Graduate Education and Research Traineeship (IGERT). Moreover, the European Research Council (ERC) has garnered attention for endorsing “Frontier Research” proposals that venture beyond the confines of established disciplinary parameters.

***Advantages***

Interdisciplinary research confers a multitude of advantages, acting as a pivotal conduit for the generative recombination processes that propel scientific advancement ([Rafols et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048733312000765)). The multifaceted issues such as climate change and resource security necessitate a confluence of expertise from the biological, physical, and social sciences ([Rylance, 2015](https://www.nature.com/articles/525313a); [Yegros-Yegros et al., 2015](https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0135095)). Innovation is frequently incubated at the nexus of diverse disciplines, a space where the introduction of novel methodologies, theoretical perspectives, and insights has the potential to fundamentally alter or refine our collective understanding. The transformative influence of big data analytics across a spectrum of scientific fields illustrates the substantial impact of such interdisciplinary engagements ([Rylance, 2015](https://www.nature.com/articles/525313a)). However, it is important to acknowledge the challenges associated with interdisciplinary research. ([Yegros-Yegros et al., 2015)](https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0135095) identify two primary categories of costs: those related to the additional resources and effort required for coordination, and those stemming from a lack of recognition for the research.

***Challenges***

The economic ramifications of interdisciplinary research are complex, encompassing direct costs like administrative and travel expenses, as well as less overt outlays due to the synchronization of disparate organizational cultures and the creation of a common vocabulary, as noted by scholars such as [Cummings & Kiesler (2005)](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0306312705055535), [Rafols (2007)](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=3200401), and [Yegros-Yegros et al. (2015)](https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0135095). The structural context of institutions exacerbates these financial burdens, presenting obstacles including limited career paths, diminished academic recognition, hurdles in publication, and biases in peer review, as documented by [Bruce et al. (2004)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0016328703001861?via%3Dihub), [Llerena & Meyer-Krahmer (2003)](https://ideas.repec.org/h/elg/eechap/2831_3.html), and [Rafols (2007)](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=3200401). Furthermore, interdisciplinary research often faces diminished esteem due to the prevalence of disciplinary silos, which marginalize unconventional methods and theories [(Barry et al., 2008)](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/03085140701760841), and the assessment of such work is frequently skewed by traditional disciplinary prejudices, complicating the development of fair evaluation standards ([Mallard et al., 2009](https://citeseerx.ist.psu.edu/document?repid=rep1&type=pdf&doi=21caf50670974faa66ae326dcb391adbdf8f01d7); [Yegros-Yegros et al., 2015](https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0135095)). Additionally, interdisciplinary projects tend to be at a disadvantage in securing funding and may experience inconsistent citation impacts ([Bromham et al., 2016](https://www.nature.com/articles/nature18315); [Porter & Rafols, 2009](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11192-008-2197-2)).

***Interdisciplinary Plant Research***

Notwithstanding the inherent challenges associated with interdisciplinary strategies, a multitude of successful plant science projects affirm their effectiveness. The realm of plant biology, teeming with a diverse spectrum of metabolites-from polyphenols to flavonoids-plays a crucial role in safeguarding plants against environmental adversaries and predators. These metabolites are not only instrumental in reinforcing plant defenses but also hold significant potential for broader applications that surpass the conventional confines of botany ([Alvira et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1385894722029564)). Their incorporation into the vanguard of technological progress highlights the remarkable versatility and practicality of plant-derived substances. For instance, the application of plant-based hard carbon (HC) in the anodes of sodium-ion batteries exemplifies the transformative impact of organic compounds on innovation and the promotion of sustainable technologies. Moreover, the development of advanced bionic structures and materials, inspired by the intelligent design principles of plant leaves, unveils the extensive opportunities that nature offers for the advancement of sustainable engineering and materials science. By harnessing the intricate and efficient natural architectures of vegetation, scientists have devised novel, nature-mimicking approaches that significantly enhance the performance and sustainability of various engineering fields. Notably, the morphology of plant leaves has become an influential model for bionic design, demonstrating nature’s ingenuity in informing and inspiring technological solutions [(Yu et al., 2023)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0079642523001135?via%3Dihub).

***Plant sentience?***

The convergence of plant science with characteristics reminiscent of the animal kingdom has precipitated an engrossing inquiry into the concept of plant sentience [(Khattar et al., 2022).](https://ethnobiomed.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s13002-022-00539-3#citeas) This line of investigation is upending the archetypical understanding of vegetative comportment and communicative mechanisms. Advancements in this field are revealing an elaborate matrix of signal transduction and adaptive responses, equipping flora to navigate and flourish within dynamic habitats. Emergent studies have illuminated the intriguing phenomenon that flora exhibits a modality of communication analogous to the vocalizations observed in the Animalia. Specifically, under conditions of duress, plants have been documented to produce acoustic emissions that serve as indicators of their physiological state and the external biotic and abiotic stresses they encounter [(Khait et al., 2023)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0092867423002623?via%3Dihub). Such discoveries underscore the complexity of plant interactions with their milieu, suggesting an extent of cognizance and reactivity that surpasses the conventional parameters of plant science.

***Interdisciplinary cooperation***

To facilitate the cross-pollination of disciplines and ameliorate the inherent challenges therein, strategic policy measures are imperative to surmount coordination barriers and institutional impediments. Moreover, a paradigm shift in disciplinary and institutional norms towards the valorization of atypical research endeavors is paramount. The enrichment of editorial consortiums within academic journals with a plurality of disciplinary perspectives, coupled with the advocacy of interdisciplinary methodologies within tertiary education systems, is instrumental in nurturing enduring cross-disciplinary partnerships [(Yegros-Yegros et al., 2015)](https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0135095). The intricate conundrums of plant science necessitate an approach steeped in interdisciplinary collaboration. The synthesis of robust solutions demands the amalgamation of varied perspectives and areas of expertise, thereby eclipsing the confines of conventional disciplinary silos and engaging a consortium of stakeholders from disparate sectors. The pivotal publication “One Hundred Important Questions Facing Plant Science: An International Perspective” delineates the scope and magnitude of the disciplinary inquiries, accentuating the imperative for a tapestry of viewpoints and specialized knowledge to address the most pressing challenges within the domain [(Armstrong et al., 2023)](https://nph.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/nph.18771). The complexity of these questions often transcends the realm of strict scientific investigation, requiring the engagement of social scientists, policy architects, non-governmental organizations, and commercial entities to devise solutions that are both comprehensive and integrative [(Matzek et al., 2014)](https://conbio.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/conl.12042).

**References**

**About the Authors**

Villő Bernád is a final-year PhD student at UCD, and a [2024 Plantae Fellow](https://plantae.org/aspb-plantae-fellows-2024/). She is focusing on the study of waterlogging stress in barley, and her research interests lie in the fields of bioinformatics and computational biology. You can find her on X: [@BernadVillo](https://twitter.com/BernadVillo).

Yueh Cho is a senior postdoc fellow at the Institute of Plant and Microbial Biology, Academia Sinica, Taiwan, and a [2024 Plantae Fellow](https://plantae.org/aspb-plantae-fellows-2024/). He is majoring in plant biology with expertise in protein homeostasis. He used Arabidopsis thaliana to mechanistically study ribosome behavior during translation and protein quality control inside the endoplasmic reticulum in response to the surrounding environment. You can find him on X:  [@YuehCho1984](https://twitter.com/YuehCho1984)

Andrew Hempton is a PhD student at the University of Washington, and a [2024 Plantae Fellow](https://plantae.org/aspb-plantae-fellows-2024/). He currently investigates the molecular and physiological mechanisms of shade induced developmental responses in plants.

# Insights into the Molecular Dynamics of Stress Physiology in Allium Crops

2,525

Total downloads

10k

Total views and downloads

Submit your idea

Select the journal/section where you want your idea to be submitted:

## About this Research Topic

Submission deadlines

Manuscript Submission Deadline 24 August 2025

This Research Topic is still accepting articles.

### Background

Understanding stress physiology and molecular response mechanisms in plants is essential for improving crop production and quality, particularly under environmental stress. Allium crops, including onions, garlic, and leeks, are vital in global agriculture but face significant challenges from biotic (disease, pest, viruses) and abiotic (drought, salinity, waterlogging, and temperature extremes and nutrient deficiency) stresses. Despite their economic and nutritional importance, the physiological and molecular mechanisms underpinning stress responses in Allium crops remain underexplored compared to other major crop species. Bridging this knowledge gap is critical to enhance sustainable production and ensure post-harvest quality.  
  
This Research Topic aims to advance our understanding of the physiological and molecular responses of Allium crops to both biotic and environmental stresses. By integrating physiological regulation, environmental interactions, plant-pathogen interactions, and sustainable practices, we seek to provide insights that can improve crop resilience, yield, and post-harvest quality. The objective is to develop a mechanistic understanding of how stress influences Allium crop production and to identify actionable strategies for mitigating these effects.  
  
This Research Topic welcomes submissions focusing on physiological and molecular insights that directly enhance our understanding of Allium crop production and quality, particularly under stress conditions. Relevant areas include, but are not limited to:  
  
• Stress Physiology and Crop Performance: Exploring the impact of abiotic stresses on Allium crop physiology and their implications for pre- and post-harvest product quality.  
  
• Disease and Pest Infection Response: Investigating physiological and molecular responses to biotic stresses, including pathogens and pests, and their management. This includes identifying pathogen-specific response pathways, plant defense mechanisms, and the role of resistance genes in mitigating biotic stress. Further emphasis is placed on understanding vector-pathogen-host dynamics, signaling pathways triggered by pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs), and effector proteins in Allium crops.  
  
• Molecular Pathology: Elucidating molecular interactions between Allium crops and pathogens, with a focus on the identification of virulence factors, plant immunity pathways, and transcriptomic and proteomic changes during infections. Studies on the epigenetic regulation of defense responses and the role of secondary metabolites in pathogen resistance are particularly encouraged.  
  
• Molecular Mechanisms: Identifying stress-responsive genes, proteins, and metabolites that regulate Allium crop production and resilience.  
  
• Physiological Regulation: Investigating the role of hormonal signaling, nutrient transport, and epigenetic modifications in stress responses.  
  
• Scaling Insights Across Biological Levels: Bridging the understanding of stress physiology from molecular, cellular, and tissue levels to the whole plant and crop scales.  
  
• Environmental Interactions: Examining how cultivation techniques and environmental factors influence Allium crop physiology and their combined effects on yield and quality.  
  
• Innovative Approaches: Leveraging transgenic technologies, CRISPR/Cas9, or other advanced molecular tools to enhance stress resilience and post-harvest product quality in Allium crops.  
  
Submissions should include robust experimental designs with quantitative analyses based on a minimum of three biological replicates. Studies must contribute novel physiological insights rather than descriptive data alone. Research focusing on transgenic or mutant lines must meet replication requirements and ensure data is derived from multiple alleles, where applicable.   
  
This Research Topic emphasizes sustainable agricultural practices, supporting SDG goals for sustainable agriculture (SDG 2), responsible consumption and production (SDG 12), and climate action (SDG 13). Studies addressing these objectives through the lens of Allium crop physiology are particularly encouraged.

![Research Topic Research topic image](https://www.frontiersin.org/image/researchtopic/69224)

## Frequently asked questions

Frontiers' Research Topics are collaborative hubs built around an emerging theme.Defined, managed, and led by renowned researchers, they bring communities together around a shared area of interest to stimulate collaboration and innovation.

Unlike section journals, which serve established specialty communities, Research Topics are pioneer hubs, responding to the evolving scientific landscape and catering to new communities.

The goal of Frontiers' publishing program is to empower research communities to actively steer the course of scientific publishing. Our program was implemented as a three-part unit with fixed field journals, flexible specialty sections, and dynamically emerging Research Topics, connecting communities of different sizes and maturity.

Research Topics originate from the scientific community. Many of our Research Topics are suggested by existing editorial board members who have identified critical challenges or areas of interest in their field.

As an editor, Research Topics will help you build your journal, as well as your community, around emerging, cutting-edge research. As research trailblazers, Research Topics attract high-quality submissions from leading experts all over the world.

A thriving Research Topic can potentially evolve into a new specialty section if there is sustained interest and a growing community around it.

Each Research Topic must be approved by the specialty chief editor, and it falls under the editorial oversight of our editorial boards, supported by our in-house research integrity team. The same standards and rigorous peer review processes apply to articles published as part of a Research Topic as for any other article we publish.

In 2023, 80% of the Research Topics we published were edited or co-edited by our editorial board members, who are already familiar with their journal's scope, ethos, and publishing model. All other topics are guest edited by leaders in their field, each vetted and formally approved by the specialty chief editor.

Publishing your article within a Research Topic with other related articles increases its discoverability and visibility, which can lead to more views, downloads, and citations. Research Topics grow dynamically as more published articles are added, causing frequent revisiting, and further visibility.

As Research Topics are multidisciplinary, they are cross-listed in several fields and section journals – increasing your reach even more and giving you the chance to expand your network and collaborate with researchers in different fields, all focusing on expanding knowledge around the same important topic.

Our larger Research Topics are also converted into ebooks and receive social media promotion from our digital marketing team.

Frontiers offers multiple article types, but it will depend on the field and section journals in which the Research Topic will be featured. The available article types for a Research Topic will appear in the drop-down menu during the submission process.

[Check available article types here](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/for-authors/article-types)

Yes, we would love to hear your ideas for a topic. Most of our Research Topics are community-led and suggested by researchers in the field. Our in-house editorial team will contact you to talk about your idea and whether you’d like to edit the topic. If you’re an early-stage researcher, we will offer you the opportunity to coordinate your topic, with the support of a senior researcher as the topic editor.

[Suggest your topic here](https://researchtopics.frontiersin.org/submit)

A team of guest editors (called topic editors) lead their Research Topic. This editorial team oversees the entire process, from the initial topic proposal to calls for participation, the peer review, and final publications.

The team may also include topic coordinators, who help the topic editors send calls for participation, liaise with topic editors on abstracts, and support contributing authors. In some cases, they can also be assigned as reviewers.

As a topic editor (TE), you will take the lead on all editorial decisions for the Research Topic, starting with defining its scope. This allows you to curate research around a topic that interests you, bring together different perspectives from leading researchers across different fields and shape the future of your field.

You will choose your team of co-editors, curate a list of potential authors, send calls for participation and oversee the peer review process, accepting or recommending rejection for each manuscript submitted.

As a topic editor, you're supported at every stage by our in-house team. You will be assigned a single point of contact to help you on both editorial and technical matters. Your topic is managed through our user-friendly online platform, and the peer review process is supported by our industry-first AI review assistant (AIRA).

If you’re an early-stage researcher, we will offer you the opportunity to coordinate your topic, with the support of a senior researcher as the topic editor. This provides you with valuable editorial experience, improving your ability to critically evaluate research articles and enhancing your understanding of the quality standards and requirements for scientific publishing, as well as the opportunity to discover new research in your field, and expand your professional network.

Yes, certificates can be issued on request. We are happy to provide a certificate for your contribution to editing a successful Research Topic.

Research Topics thrive on collaboration and their multi-disciplinary approach around emerging, cutting-edge themes, attract leading researchers from all over the world.

As a topic editor, you can set the timeline for your Research Topic, and we will work with you at your pace. Typically, Research Topics are online and open for submissions within a few weeks and remain open for participation for 6 – 12 months. Individual articles within a Research Topic are published as soon as they are ready.

Find out more [about our Research Topics](https://www.frontiersin.org/about/research-topics)

Our fee support program ensures that all articles that pass peer review, including those published in Research Topics, can benefit from open access – regardless of the author's field or funding situation.

Authors and institutions with insufficient funding can apply for a discount on their publishing fees. A [fee support application form](https://frontiersin.qualtrics.com/jfe/form/SV_9YqUCmY73k4Gm0u) is available on our website.

In line with our mission to promote healthy lives on a healthy planet, we do not provide printed materials. All our articles and ebooks are available under a CC-BY license, so you can share and print copies.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Collaborative efforts between biology, chemistry, and physics enhance understanding of plant stress responses, fostering innovation in stress mitigation strategies and sustainable practices.
Engagement with agricultural industries ensures research findings translate into practical applications, improving crop resilience and productivity through technology transfer and shared expertise.
International partnerships aimed at addressing food insecurity leverage diverse expertise to develop resilient agricultural systems capable of withstanding climate change and environmental stresses.
